V1.5 is now live. V1.0 was a simple demo focused on "functionality" — once I confirmed that every feature worked, I moved quickly into a design upgrade.

I've incorporated the community's requested changes and are unveiling a fully upgraded RISE Screener.

It's organized into 7 tabs:

1. Overview — Get a complete exchange snapshot, including TVL/Volume/OI along with Top OI / Volume / Gainers / Losers.
2. Markets — Price and 24h change for every market listed on RISEx, plus funding rates and max leverage.
3. Fees/Liquidations — Track liquidations on RISEx and the fees generated from trading.
4. Traders — A window into RISEx's top traders. Because RISEx is fully on-chain, you can see the 24h volume / unrealized PnL / OI of the top n traders. Dive into the details — their maker/taker activity, estimated liquidation prices, and more.
5. Flows — Spot new flow through deposit/withdrawal data moving in and out of RISEx.
6. Network — For anyone curious about RISE Chain: live-data-driven animations and explanations. Learn the theory behind why RISE Chain is so fast.
7. Explorer — Look up RISE Chain blocks and individual txs, and feel the speed for yourself. (You'll probably struggle just to follow it with your eyes.)

You can switch between light/dark mode using the toggle at the top.

Copy trading on @risextrade  

Just follow the top-performing wallets.

Continuously monitor the top 3 wallets by top PnL, OI, and volume on RISEx. Or, if you prefer, you can search for a specific wallet and copy it.

In this video, we search for a specific wallet and demonstrate copy trading actually working — with near-zero copy latency.

2. Run your own bot. @risextrade 

You can build bots based on the most well-known and popular strategies — using them individually or in combination. Each strategy comes with an explanation, along with guidance on entry/exit (buy/sell) points.

At launch, initial assets will support three options — BTC, ETH, and SOL — and you can fine-tune your bot by adjusting detailed parameters. 

And the backtesting is insane! 
It provides backtesting based on real data, factoring in exchange fees of 3 bps and typical slippage of around 1 bps.

If you had run the bot a week or a month ago, you can check exactly when and at what time the buys and sells would have occurred — and keep improving your bot through further tuning.

And a bit further down the line, we also plan to open up free-code-based bot building.

AI Agent-Based Trading Terminal: TERMINUS

We've started developing an AI agent-based trading terminal. It supports AI-driven trading, and also enables real-time PnL management as well as buying and selling agents.

As a first step, we're preparing order routing to @risextrade, and our AI functionality uses @flock_io API Platform as an intermediary. If users have their own OpenAI or Claude API keys, they can also connect their personal AI. The features currently in demo are as follows:

1. AI-Assisted Bot Setup

- Turn your own strategies into bots by normalizing them into a standardized format. (Base AI usage credits are provided for agent setup. Additional payment is required if you exceed your AI call allowance.)
- Support for backtesting, paper trading, and live trading of the bots you create. (Paper trading runs against Risex's real on-chain order book for high-fidelity simulation.)
- Manage each agent's PnL and history, with AI-assisted improvement suggestions based on that history.

2. AI Agent Marketplace

- A marketplace where you can buy, sell, and pay-per-call AI agents.
- Full transparency through publicly visible real-time history, PnL, and other metrics.

3. Gamification

- Random Spin — a randomizer for position / leverage / side.

Are there any features you'd like to see added? If so, we'd love to take your suggestions into account.

After a few conversations, I've really grown fond of this guy. And I also really like the way he's building things. @risextrade  might not be the biggest airdrop ever, but it will be the best option for you in the current phase.

Someone asked me why I'm bullish on @risextrade , and here are a few reasons I can quickly list:
- CLOB is fully on-chain, which is what I value most. It's the highest industry standard right now.

- It integrates directly into @risechain , an L2 token of ETH. What does this mean? You own a fully on-chain CLOB integrated directly into an ETH L2 token, which opens the door to access to capital and integration capabilities. Many people will argue, "Oh, isn't L2 dead?" Actually, L2 isn't dead; it's just being misused.

You have a high-speed L2 EVM that can integrate a fully on-chain CLOB token; just thinking about it allows you to build so many cool things on it.

Okay, so how is it different from Lighter?

Lighter is an Ethereum L2 token with custom ZK circuits for verifiable matching and liquidations. Essentially, they don't have an EVM yet and are still in the development process. Whether their EVM, once completed, will be able to communicate atomically with their CLOB is still unconfirmed, which is crucial for opening up the possibility of integration.

What does Risex need to succeed?

- Liquidity is a prerequisite for successful peer-to-peer marketing, especially for a CLOB; launching liquidity for the markets is even more necessary and expensive.

Therefore, attracting users and market makers is paramount. No matter how good a product is in terms of design and architecture, without liquidity and users, it will ultimately fail.

That's enough. My final piece of advice: Don't just read and listen, try it out. Research is good, but don't just stop at reading and listening. Using and experiencing a new product yourself will give you the most accurate and objective perspective.

@risextrade
The Core Idea Behind Market-Making BotsThese are automated liquidity providers. They don't try to predict if price goes up or down. Instead, they make money by capturing the bid-ask spread (the small gap between buy and sell prices) while keeping the orderbook healthy. The bot places buy (bid) orders slightly below the current price.  
It places sell (ask) orders slightly above the current price.  
When both sides get filled (someone buys from your sell or sells to your buy), you pocket the spread difference — repeatedly, 24/7.  
On RISEx’s ultra-fast orderbook (sub-3ms updates), this happens smoothly with minimal risk of getting run over. 
https://t.co/SltJBB35fx
They adjust orders dynamically as price moves, cancel/replace to stay competitive, and often include safety stops to avoid big directional exposure.

what do all these mean for perp DEXs out there?

CFTC’s historic regulatory breakthrough has completely rewritten the landscape for perpetual decentralized exchanges Perp DEXs On-chain platforms like @risextrade, @HyperliquidX, now face an entirely new set of market dynamics, competitive threats, and structural opportunities

now you might be wondering what these means for perp dexs, are they gonna start losing momentum?

the CFTC ruling does not kill DeFi derivatives, instead, it formalizes a permanent, two-sided market split,

while legal onshore options like Coinbase and Kalshi will capture risk-averse institutional money, they cannot replicate the core structural mechanics that make Perp DEXs attractive to native crypto traders

here's the thing,Onchain platforms possess foundational structural advantages that centralized, regulated entities cannot legally or technologically offer

1: Instant Listing of "Long Tail" Assets: Regulated US platforms must clear every single asset through a grueling case by case review process

 If a brand new token launches, Coinbase may take months or years to approve a perp for it, In contrast, Hyperliquid’s HIP-3 framework allows permissionless listing of any asset with a price feed instantly

2: No Counterparty or Credit Risk: the FTX collapse is a good example

but on platforms like RISEx, the liquidations, order matching, and safety vaults are completely transparent and automated on the blockchain, traders can audit the exchange’s solvency in real time, rather than blindly trusting a corporation's internal books

3: Global Pre-IPO and Synthetic Asset Access: DeFi platforms are morphing into comprehensive, 24/7 global synthetic asset engines, for example, when SpaceX listed on Nasdaq in early June 2026, Hyperliquid's 'SPCX' stock-linked perpetual contract instantly exploded to $1.4 billion in a single day's trading volume
 A US regulated platform would face massive SEC roadblocks trying to offer high-leverage synthetic equities to retail traders

The @risextrade - Most people see new listings.
I see a stress test for market infrastructure.

$VVV and $LIT are now live on RISEx with up to 3x leverage. The interesting part is not the assets.

It's whether a low latency orderbook can maintain efficient price discovery when volatility and leverage and and liquidity all interact simultaneously.

That's where market architecture gets tested. Not during quiet markets. During active ones. @hai_rise 

https://t.co/6ci6aUB3ir

The @risechain @risextrade - Most people describe Shreds as a faster confirmation mechanism. 

I think that's underselling what's actually happening.

Shreds transform blockchain execution from a batch system into a streaming system.  

1. Traditional blockchains operate like batch processors.

🔹Transactions wait.
🔹A block forms.
🔹The block finishes.
🔹Then the network responds. 

State updates arrive in bursts.  

2. Shreds change the timing model. 

Instead of waiting for a completed block, execution state is propagated continuously as work is performed.

Confirmation becomes a stream not an event.  

3. This matters because latency is not just a UX problem. It's a coordination problem.

Market makers and liquidators and arbitrage systems and traders all depend on how quickly shared state updates propagate through the network.  

4. The interesting part is not simply that confirmations arrive in ~1ms. 

It's that economically secured state information becomes available before block completion.

That reduces the gap between execution and awareness.  

5. Viewed this way Shreds are not merely making blocks faster. 

They are reducing the importance of the block as the primary unit of interaction.

The network starts behaving less like a sequence of batches and more like a continuous real time system.

That's a deeper architectural shift than speed alone.

The @risechain @risextrade - Most people think liquidity is capital. 

I think that's the wrong mental model. Liquidity is coordination.

And that's why the CLOB vs RFQ debate matters far beyond trading. 

1. A dealer in an RFQ system can provide liquidity.
But that liquidity only exists when someone asks for it.

🔹It's private.
🔹Request based.
🔹Temporary. 

Useful for execution Not useful for coordination. 

2. Public orderbooks work differently. Liquidity exists before the trade.

🔹Anyone can see it.
🔹Anyone can reference it.
🔹Anyone can build against it. 

The book becomes shared market knowledge. 

3. That's the real value of a CLOB. Not tighter spreads and Not more volume.

A shared coordination layer where traders and vaults and liquidators and arbitrageurs and protocols all react to the same state. 

4. Once liquidity becomes public state. 

🔹Price discovery becomes shared.
🔹Risk becomes observable.
🔹Strategies become composable.
🔹Capital becomes programmable. 

The market starts coordinating itself. 

5. This is why I think RISEx's architecture is interesting. The orderbook is not being treated as a trading interface.

It's being treated as infrastructure Because the most valuable thing liquidity creates is not execution.

It's coordination.

The @risextrade - Everyone sees market makers as liquidity. The deeper role is much more interesting.

Market makers are actually a chain's first line of latency infrastructure.  

1. When people hear ➔ Arbital is live on RISEx.

They think ➔ 

🔹More liquidity.
🔹Tighter spreads Thats True.

But that's only the surface layer. 

2. A market maker's real job is not providing liquidity. 
It's continuously updating prices as new information arrives.

The faster information becomes reflected in markets.
The more efficient those markets become.  

3. This creates an interesting requirement. A market maker can only be as responsive as the infrastructure beneath it.

🔹Slow state updates create stale quotes.
🔹Stale quotes create wider spreads.
🔹Wider spreads create worse markets. 

4. This is why RISEx and market makers are naturally connected. RISEx is optimizing for low latency state updates. 

Arbital is optimizing for low latency price updates Both are solving different sides of the same problem.  

5. Most discussions focus on liquidity depth. But market quality is really the interaction between ➔ 

🔹 Infrastructure latency.
🔹 Price discovery speed.
🔹 Liquidity efficiency.

Remove one and the system degrades. 

6. That's what makes the Arbital integration interesting. It's not simply adding more capital.

It's adding a participant specifically designed to exploit the advantages of a low latency market architecture.

The real test of RISEx is not whether market makers join. It's whether the infrastructure lets them operate in ways that are not possible elsewhere.  @hai_rise

Here is what "1ms fast" looks like in real-time, just check how quick these transactions are clearing on @risextrade executing orderbook updates and instant settlements.

Here is the actual tech making that instant speed happen under the @risechain hood:
 
▪️PEVM (Parallel EVM) - executing multiple trades at the exact same time across different computer cores, so nothing gets stuck waiting in a slow line
▪️CBP (Continuous Block Pipeline) -  processing transactions continuously as they arrive, instead of waiting for full blocks to form
▪️Shreds - breaking large blocks into tiny "mini-blocks," giving users near-instant transaction confirmations
▪️Based Sequencing-  outsourcing transaction ordering to Ethereum’s own validators, meaning you get crazy speed without sacrificing security or decentralization

We might not see any of this when trading, but this is exactly the idea - so that UI for regular users is so smooth that it feels exactly like trading on a centralized exchange, but instead being fully on-chain.

The RISEx and Arbital Partnership Impact on Liquidity

The integration of Arbital with RISEx signifies a pivotal development for decentralized finance. 

By bringing systematic institutional market-making to @risextrade, the partnership aims to address common liquidity challenges faced by DEXs, ultimately leading to a more efficient and robust trading environment.

Key Impacts on Market Liquidity
1. Spread Compression and Cost Efficiency
Arbital's automated strategies are designed to maintain tight bid-ask spreads, which directly benefits traders by reducing transaction costs. 

The continuous quoting mechanism ensures that orders are consistently updated, reflecting current market conditions and minimizing the gap between buying and selling prices. 

This systematic approach contrasts sharply with the often wider and more inconsistent spreads found in less actively managed decentralized markets.

2. Enhanced Orderbook Depth
The partnership fosters a significant increase in orderbook depth. 
@arbital_xyz 's engine is optimized for maker-only execution, meaning it primarily adds liquidity to the orderbook rather than consuming it. 

Coupled with robust inventory management and risk controls, market makers can sustain their presence in the market, providing stable and substantial depth across various price levels. This resilience helps prevent liquidity evaporation during volatile periods.

3. Slippage Reduction for Large Trades
For large-volume traders, slippage is a critical concern on decentralized platforms. The combined capabilities of RISEx's high TPS and Arbital's rapid order replenishment significantly mitigate this issue. 

When a large trade consumes existing liquidity, Arbital bots can instantaneously re-populate the orderbook, ensuring that subsequent trades encounter minimal price impact. This creates a more favorable environment for institutional participants.

4. Incentive Alignment and Capital Efficiency
The synergy between RISEx's native features and Arbital's strategic execution creates a highly capital-efficient ecosystem. 

Users providing liquidity through Arbital can simultaneously benefit from RISEx's AutoYield feature, earning returns on their collateral. 

Furthermore, Arbital simplifies participation in RISEx's incentive programs, attracting
capital that is motivated by both trading profits and ecosystem rewards, thereby fostering deeper and more sustainable liquidity.

RISEx (@risextrade) is now live on @cedeflow . Small headline, structurally important.

Here's the thing nobody says out loud about "permissionless market making": it's a claim until the integration layer exists. A CLOB lets anyone post a resting order, in theory. In practice, only the firms that can stomach custom API work, low-level signing, and latency tuning ever actually connect. The book is only as deep as the makers who show up, and makers show up where it's easy to plug a bot in.

That's exactly the gap Cede Flow closes. The team behind it (Cede Hub, $1B+ in volume, backed by GSR) is turning "you could make markets here" into "you can run an MM bot here in a few clicks."

Why this matters specifically for a young venue: in week one of mainnet, the XLP vault was effectively the sole counterparty on RISEx, holding most of the OI and all the inventory risk. The way a venue graduates from one-vault-holds-everything to a competitive book is by growing the maker set. And you grow the maker set by collapsing the activation energy to connect. Tooling is the mechanism.

So this isn't just an integration announcement. It's the supply side of the orderbook getting easier to join. More independent makers, more competition on spread, less concentration risk, on a fully onchain CLOB on RISE Chain where every quote they post is verifiable state.

Permissionless market making only counts once someone builds the on-ramp. This is the on-ramp.

https://t.co/FhwLOzwoCK

RISEx (@risextrade) today, and the microstructure flipped from last week.

$35.1M volume (-15.9%), $9.6M open interest (+51%). Volume/OI ratio compressed to 3.7x, down from the high-churn 9x reads earlier. That move matters: volume cooling while OI jumps by half means positions are sticking instead of flipping. Less day-trading noise, more held exposure. The venue is shifting from pure price-discovery churn toward actual carry.

The OI chart makes it obvious, that's a clean step-up to a new high, BTC and ETH leading the stack.

Funding tells the rest. The whole top of book is printing negative: $BTC -0.014%, $ETH -0.049%, $HYPE -0.023%, $SOL -0.021% on the 8h. Shorts are paying longs across the board on a green day ($ETH +4%, $SOL +4.7%). That's classic short positioning leaning into a bounce and getting taxed for it. Watch HYPE specifically: 8h funding negative but 7d average still positive, sentiment turned intraday and the rate is catching up live.

The clean part: this is a fully onchain CLOB on RISE Chain. Every funding print, every OI tick is verifiable state, not an exchange-reported figure you take on trust. The dashboard is just reading the chain.
